section.title: Ahijah's Prophecy
section.sectionRationale: Following the tragic episode with the man of God from Judah, chapter 14 returns to Jeroboam directly. When his son Abijah falls ill, Jeroboam sends his wife in disguise to Ahijah, the very prophet through whom the LORD had first given him the kingdom, 1 Kings 11:29-39, hoping for a favorable word. Instead, though blind with age, Ahijah recognizes her by the LORD's own revelation and pronounces total judgment on Jeroboam's house because he has done more evil than all before him, making molten images and abandoning the LORD who raised him up. The child will die the moment his mother crosses the threshold home, a sign that though this one heir at least will be mourned and buried, the rest of the dynasty will be utterly cut off. The chapter then closes out Jeroboam's and Rehoboam's reigns with matching regnal summaries, noting Judah's own slide into high places, sacred pillars, and Asherah poles, and Shishak's humiliating raid on Jerusalem's treasures.
section.thesis: The LORD who raised Jeroboam up now announces, through the very prophet who first anointed him, the total collapse of his house because he has abandoned the LORD for idols, and the same chapter shows that Judah under Rehoboam, though it keeps the LORD's chosen city and temple, is sliding into the same idolatry that will bring its own painful chastening.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Jeroboam sends his disguised wife to Ahijah, verses 1 to 6; Ahijah's prophecy of judgment on Jeroboam's house, verses 7 to 16; the child's death and the summary of Jeroboam's reign, verses 17 to 20; Rehoboam's reign and Judah's own sin, verses 21 to 24; and Shishak's raid and the close of Rehoboam's reign, verses 25 to 31.

units.label: Jeroboam sends his disguised wife
units.note: seeking a word about the dying child

verses.text: At that time Abijah son of Jeroboam became ill,

and Jeroboam said to his wife, “Now get up, disguise yourself so they will not recognize you as my wife, and go to Shiloh. For Ahijah the {{navi|prophet}} is there; it was he who spoke about my kingship over this people.
Take with you ten loaves of bread, some cakes, and a jar of honey, and go to him. He will tell you what will become of the boy.”
Jeroboam’s wife did as instructed; she arose and went to Shiloh and arrived at Ahijah’s house. Now Ahijah could not see, for his eyes were dim because of his age.

But the LORD had said to Ahijah, “Behold, the wife of Jeroboam is coming to ask you about her son, for he is ill. You are to say such and such to her, because when she arrives, she will be disguised.”
So when Ahijah heard the sound of her feet entering the door, he said, “Come in, wife of Jeroboam! Why are you disguised? For I have been sent to you with bad news.

units.label: Ahijah's prophecy of judgment
units.note: the house of Jeroboam cut off

verses.text: Go, tell Jeroboam that this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘I raised you up from among the people and appointed you ruler over My people Israel.

I tore the kingdom away from the house of David and gave it to you. But you have not been like My servant David, who kept My commandments and followed Me with all his {{lev|heart}}, doing only what was right in My eyes.
You have done more evil than all who came before you. You have proceeded to make for yourself other gods and molten images to provoke Me, and you have flung Me behind your back.

Because of all this, behold, I am bringing disaster on the house of Jeroboam: I will cut off from Jeroboam every male, both slave and free, in Israel; I will burn up the house of Jeroboam as one burns up dung until it is gone!
Anyone belonging to Jeroboam who dies in the city will be eaten by dogs, and anyone who dies in the field will be eaten by the birds of the air.’ For the LORD has spoken.
As for you, get up and go home. When your feet enter the city, the child will die.

All Israel will mourn for him and bury him. For this is the only one belonging to Jeroboam who will receive a proper burial, because only in him has the LORD, the God of Israel, found any good in the house of Jeroboam.
Moreover, the LORD will raise up for Himself a king over Israel who will cut off the house of Jeroboam. This is the day, yes, even today!

For the LORD will strike Israel as a reed is shaken in the water. He will uproot Israel from this good land that He gave their fathers, and He will scatter them beyond the Euphrates, because they have made their Asherah poles, provoking the LORD to anger.
So He will give Israel over on account of the sins Jeroboam has committed and has caused Israel to commit.”

units.label: The child's death and Jeroboam's end

verses.text: Then Jeroboam’s wife got up and departed for Tirzah, and as soon as she stepped over the threshold of the house, the boy died.
And they buried him, and all Israel mourned for him, according to the {{dabar|word}} that the LORD had spoken through His servant Ahijah the prophet.
As for the rest of the acts of Jeroboam, how he waged war and how he reigned, they are indeed written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
And the length of Jeroboam’s reign was twenty-two years, and he rested with his fathers, and his son Nadab reigned in his place.

Meanwhile, Rehoboam son of Solomon reigned in Judah. He was forty-one years old when he became king, and he reigned seventeen years in Jerusalem, the city the LORD had chosen from all the tribes of Israel in which to put His Name. His mother’s name was Naamah the Ammonite.
And Judah did evil in the sight of the LORD, and by the sins they committed they provoked Him to jealous anger more than all their fathers had done.
They also built for themselves {{bamah|high places}}, sacred pillars, and Asherah poles on every high hill and under every green tree.
There were even male shrine prostitutes in the land. They imitated all the abominations of the nations the LORD had driven out before the Israelites.

units.label: Shishak's raid and the close of Rehoboam's reign

verses.text: In the fifth year of Rehoboam’s reign, Shishak king of Egypt attacked Jerusalem.
He seized the treasures of the house of the LORD and of the royal palace. He took everything, including all the gold shields that Solomon had made.
Then King Rehoboam made bronze shields in their place and committed them to the care of the captains of the guard on duty at the entrance to the royal palace.
And whenever the king entered the house of the LORD, the guards would bear the shields, and later they would return them to the guardroom.
As for the rest of the acts of Rehoboam, along with all that he did, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
There was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam throughout their days.
And Rehoboam rested with his fathers and was buried with them in the City of David; his mother’s name was Naamah the Ammonite. And his son Abijam reigned in his place.

navi.gloss: Prophet, one who speaks on behalf of God. Ahijah is the same navi through whom the LORD originally tore ten tribes from Solomon's house and gave them to Jeroboam, 1 Kings 11:29-39, and now the same navi announces that Jeroboam's own house will be cut off.

lev.gloss: Heart, the inner person, will, and character. David is held up as the standard because he followed the LORD with all his lev, doing only what was right; Jeroboam is condemned precisely because his heart did not match David's undivided devotion.

dabar.gloss: Word, matter, or thing spoken. Ahijah's prophecy is fulfilled exactly according to the dabar the LORD had spoken, the moment the mother's feet cross the threshold, underscoring that God's spoken word never fails, whether given as blessing or as judgment.

bamah.gloss: High place, an elevated site used for worship, often unauthorized or idolatrous. Judah under Rehoboam builds these alongside sacred pillars and Asherah poles, showing that Israel's northern sin of illegitimate worship has already spread south into the very kingdom that kept Jerusalem and the temple.

basic.context: Chapter 13 closed with Jeroboam still refusing to repent even after seeing the LORD's power firsthand. Chapter 14 opens with sickness striking his own household, his son Abijah falls gravely ill. Jeroboam sends his wife in disguise to Ahijah, the prophet through whom the LORD had originally promised him the kingdom, 1 Kings 11:29-39, hoping perhaps for a softer word than he expects. Though blind with age, Ahijah is told beforehand by the LORD exactly who is coming, and he delivers a devastating prophecy, because Jeroboam has done more evil than any king before him, making idols and abandoning the LORD who raised him from nothing, his entire house will be cut off, and his son will die the moment his mother returns home. The chapter then closes both Jeroboam's and Rehoboam's reigns with standard regnal summaries, revealing that Judah, too, despite keeping Jerusalem and the temple, has fallen into high places and Asherah worship, provoking Shishak's humiliating raid on the temple treasures.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a specific prophetic word and its exact fulfillment, it establishes the enduring pattern used throughout Kings, that every king is measured by faithfulness to the LORD alone, and that no dynasty, however established by God's own word, is safe from judgment when it turns to idols. The account is not prescribing disguise or deception as a strategy for receiving a word from God, and Rehoboam's failures show this same principle applies in Judah, not only Israel.
basic.originalLanguages: Ahijah, called simply the navi, prophet, throughout the chapter, is the same man who originally tore the kingdom from Solomon's house and gave ten tribes to Jeroboam by the dabar, word, of the LORD, 1 Kings 11:29-39; now that same dabar pronounces judgment because Jeroboam did not follow the LORD with all his lev, heart, as David had. The chapter also names the bamah, high places, that Judah under Rehoboam built, the same illegitimate worship sites that will recur as the standing measure of covenant unfaithfulness throughout the two kingdoms.
basic.typology: Ahijah's prophecy, given precisely and fulfilled to the letter the moment the mother's feet cross the threshold, again testifies that God's word never fails, whether in blessing, as when He first gave Jeroboam the kingdom, or in judgment, as when He now takes it from his line. The contrast between David's undivided heart and Jeroboam's is picked up throughout Kings and points toward the perfectly devoted heart of Christ, the true Son of David who never once turned to another god.
basic.christ: David is repeatedly held up in this chapter as the standard Jeroboam failed to meet, a man who followed the LORD with all his heart. Jesus is the true Son of David who fulfills that standard perfectly, loving the Father with undivided heart and full obedience where every other king, north and south, fell short. Where Jeroboam's house is cut off for its idolatry, Christ's kingdom is established forever, because His heart never wavered from the Father, Luke 1:32-33, Hebrews 1:8.
basic.god: God is sovereign over kingship, having given Jeroboam the throne and now having the full right to take it from his family. He is also perfectly informed and unhindered by human effort to conceal the truth, telling blind Ahijah exactly who is coming and why. And He remains ultimately good even in fierce judgment, honoring Abijah, the one member of Jeroboam's house in whom any good was found, with a proper burial and Israel's mourning, even while the rest of the family faces total destruction.
basic.crossReferences: First Kings 11:29-39 records Ahijah's original prophecy giving Jeroboam the kingdom, now reversed because of his unfaithfulness. Second Kings 17 later explains Israel's exile as the outworking of exactly this pattern of sin. Second Chronicles 12 gives further detail on Shishak's invasion of Judah. And Deuteronomy 28 sets out the covenant blessings and curses that frame both Jeroboam's and Rehoboam's fates.
basic.oneStory: Jeroboam receives the kingdom by God's own word and loses it by his own idolatry, while Judah, though it keeps the temple and the line of David, is shown sliding into the very same sins. The whole account presses forward the Bible's larger story that no human structure or heritage secures faithfulness apart from a heart wholly devoted to the LORD, a devotion finally and perfectly realized only in Christ, the true Son of David.

context.literary: The chapter is built in two parallel regnal summaries, Jeroboam's, verses 19-20, and Rehoboam's, verses 21-31, framing the central prophetic scene so that both northern and southern kings are held to the same standard. The disguise motif heightens dramatic irony, Ahijah's blindness is more than compensated by the LORD's own sight, so that the attempted concealment fails before the wife even speaks.
context.historical: Shiloh had been an early home of the ark and tabernacle, Joshua 18, giving Ahijah's presence there a certain old covenant gravity even generations later. The reference to disguise and travel by a queen without royal entourage suggests genuine anxiety to avoid detection. Shishak, the Egyptian pharaoh Sheshonq the first, is independently attested in Egyptian records, and his campaign into Judah around Rehoboam's fifth year is corroborated by an inscription at Karnak, lending firm historical anchoring to this account.
context.canonical: This chapter establishes the fixed pattern the rest of Kings will follow for the northern kingdom, dynasties repeatedly cut off for idolatry, while Judah's parallel account of high places and Shishak's raid begins the long, slower story of decline and partial reform running through Rehoboam's, and eventually all of Judah's, kings until the exile.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD who raises up kings also has full authority to bring their houses down when they abandon Him for idols, using Ahijah, the very prophet who first announced Jeroboam's rise, to announce his fall, and to show in the same breath that Judah is not exempt from the same drift into false worship.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific act of disguise, a specific prophetic word, and a specific act of judgment, it does not prescribe disguise as a spiritual practice nor guarantee that every sick child is a sign of covenant judgment. What endures is the principle that God measures kings, and by extension all who lead or represent His people, by wholehearted devotion, and that idolatry has real, generational consequences.

debates.question: Why does Ahijah's true prophetic word come with such severe judgment on an entire household?

views.label: Corporate solidarity in ancient household and dynastic structures
views.case: In the ancient Near East, and consistently in the Old Testament, a king's household stood or fell together as a unit; the judgment reflects the corporate nature of dynastic accountability rather than treating each family member as individually and equally guilty, a pattern seen also in Achan's family, Joshua 7.

views.label: The judgment specifically targets a persistent, entrenched pattern of national idolatry
views.case: The text emphasizes that Jeroboam caused Israel to commit sin, verses 15-16, so the severity answers not a single act but an entrenched system of false worship that would otherwise continue indefinitely through his descendants if the dynasty continued.

debates.bearing: Either reading keeps the judgment tied to real, national covenant unfaithfulness rather than arbitrary severity, and both anticipate the New Testament's fuller revelation that ultimately each person answers individually for sin, Ezekiel 18, while God still works out corporate and generational consequences within history.

debates.question: Why is Abijah alone honored with mourning and burial, while the rest of Jeroboam's household faces disgrace, verse 13?

views.label: Because some good was found in him personally
views.case: The text states plainly that only in him has the LORD found any good in the house of Jeroboam, suggesting some personal quality or faithfulness distinguishing him from the rest of the family.

views.label: As a mark of God's mercy amid deserved judgment
views.case: Even in pronouncing total judgment, the LORD singles out one for honorable burial and national mourning, showing that His justice is never without particular, personal mercy even within a sweeping act of judgment.

debates.bearing: Both readings show that God's judgment, however sweeping, remains personally attentive and never wholly indiscriminate, a pattern of justice tempered with mercy that runs throughout Scripture.

study.originalLanguages: Ahijah, called simply the navi, prophet, throughout the chapter, is the same man who originally tore the kingdom from Solomon's house and gave ten tribes to Jeroboam by the dabar, word, of the LORD, 1 Kings 11:29-39; now that same dabar pronounces judgment because Jeroboam did not follow the LORD with all his lev, heart, as David had. The chapter also names the bamah, high places, that Judah under Rehoboam built, the same illegitimate worship sites that will recur as the standing measure of covenant unfaithfulness throughout the two kingdoms.

typology.type: Ahijah's word that gave and now takes away the kingdom, fulfilled exactly
typology.fulfillment: The certainty of every word of God, whether promise or judgment, anchoring confidence in all that God has spoken concerning Christ's eternal kingdom, Luke 1:32-33.

typology.type: David's undivided heart held up as the standard Jeroboam failed
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Son of David, who loved the Father with a perfectly undivided heart and fulfilled all righteousness, Hebrews 10:7.

typology.type: Judgment falling on an entire house because of its head's unfaithfulness
typology.fulfillment: Contrasted with Christ, the greater Head whose perfect righteousness is credited to all who are united to Him by faith, Romans 5:18-19.

study.christ: David is repeatedly held up in this chapter as the standard Jeroboam failed to meet, a man who followed the LORD with all his heart. Jesus is the true Son of David who fulfills that standard perfectly, loving the Father with undivided heart and full obedience where every other king, north and south, fell short. Where Jeroboam's house is cut off for its idolatry, Christ's kingdom is established forever, because His heart never wavered from the Father, Luke 1:32-33, Hebrews 1:8.
study.god: God is sovereign over kingship, having given Jeroboam the throne and now having the full right to take it from his family. He is also perfectly informed and unhindered by human effort to conceal the truth, telling blind Ahijah exactly who is coming and why. And He remains ultimately good even in fierce judgment, honoring Abijah, the one member of Jeroboam's house in whom any good was found, with a proper burial and Israel's mourning, even while the rest of the family faces total destruction.

crossReferences.note: Ahijah's original prophecy giving Jeroboam ten tribes of the kingdom, now reversed because of his unfaithfulness.
Looks back on Jeroboam's sin as the pattern that eventually brought Israel's exile.
Gives a fuller account of Shishak's invasion of Judah and Rehoboam's humbling before the LORD.
Sets out the covenant curse of a kingdom's downfall for unfaithfulness, the backdrop for both Jeroboam's and Rehoboam's failures.

study.oneStory: Jeroboam receives the kingdom by God's own word and loses it by his own idolatry, while Judah, though it keeps the temple and the line of David, is shown sliding into the very same sins. The whole account presses forward the Bible's larger story that no human structure or heritage secures faithfulness apart from a heart wholly devoted to the LORD, a devotion finally and perfectly realized only in Christ, the true Son of David.

quiz.q: Why did Jeroboam send his wife to Ahijah in disguise?

options.text: So that Ahijah would not recognize her as Jeroboam's wife and might give a more favorable word about their sick son, verse 2.
options.note: The text gives this exact reason for the disguise.

options.text: Because Jeroboam feared Ahijah would refuse to see the queen at all.
options.note: The text gives concealment of her identity, not refusal of an audience, as the reason.

options.text: Because it was a normal custom for all visitors to Ahijah.
options.note: This was a deliberate, specific concealment, not a general custom.

options.text: Jeroboam did not send her in disguise, she went openly as herself.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly instructs her to disguise herself.

quiz.q: How did Ahijah, though blind, know it was Jeroboam's wife at the door?

options.text: The LORD had already told him she was coming and disguised, so he was prepared before she even spoke, verses 5-6.
options.note: The text explicitly says the LORD revealed this to Ahijah beforehand.

options.text: He recognized her voice from a past meeting.
options.note: The text credits direct revelation from the LORD, not recognition of her voice.

options.text: One of his servants secretly told him who was arriving.
options.note: No servant is mentioned informing him; the LORD Himself revealed it.

options.text: He never actually realized who she was.
options.note: Verse 6 shows Ahijah immediately addressing her by name as Jeroboam's wife.

quiz.q: What is the LORD's central charge against Jeroboam in Ahijah's prophecy, verses 7-9?

options.text: That although the LORD raised him up and tore the kingdom from David's house to give it to him, Jeroboam has done more evil than all before him, making other gods and idols and turning his back on the LORD.
options.note: This is the exact substance of the charge in verses 7-9.

options.text: That Jeroboam had failed to build a temple in Israel.
options.note: The charge is idolatry and abandoning the LORD, not the absence of a temple.

options.text: That Jeroboam had married a foreign wife.
options.note: His marriage is not the stated charge; his idolatry is.

options.text: That Jeroboam had actually been faithful and this is a false accusation.
options.note: The prophecy is a true and severe charge of unfaithfulness, not a false accusation.

quiz.q: What sign confirms that Ahijah's prophecy of judgment is certain?

options.text: The child will die the very moment his mother's feet cross the threshold of their home, exactly as spoken, verses 12 and 17.
options.note: Verse 17 records the child's death happening exactly as Ahijah foretold.

options.text: Jeroboam will be struck ill himself immediately.
options.note: The stated sign concerns the child's death, not Jeroboam's own illness.

options.text: A great famine will strike Israel that same day.
options.note: No famine is mentioned as the confirming sign in this passage.

options.text: No sign or confirmation is given, it remains purely a future threat.
options.note: The child's death is given as an immediate, specific, fulfilled sign.

quiz.q: What does the account of Judah under Rehoboam, verses 21-24, reveal alongside the judgment on Jeroboam?

options.text: That Judah, despite keeping Jerusalem and the temple, also did evil and built high places, sacred pillars, and Asherah poles, provoking the LORD, showing that having the right institutions does not guarantee true faithfulness.
options.note: Verses 22-24 detail Judah's own slide into the same kinds of idolatry.

options.text: That Judah was entirely faithful in contrast to Israel's total apostasy.
options.note: The text explicitly says Judah did evil and provoked the LORD more than their fathers had, not that they were faithful.

options.text: That Rehoboam personally led Judah in flawless obedience to the LORD.
options.note: The text records Judah's sin under Rehoboam, not flawless obedience.

options.text: That Judah had no religious practices at all during this period.
options.note: The text details specific, extensive false worship practices that Judah adopted.

questions: Jeroboam hoped a disguise might soften bad news he suspected was coming. Where do you try to manage or control how you receive from God rather than simply coming honestly before Him?
Ahijah could not see with his physical eyes, yet the LORD gave him full clarity about what was happening. Where do you need to trust God's word over your own limited perception of a situation?

God's charge against Jeroboam was that he had been raised up and given everything, yet turned to idols anyway. What has God given you that you are tempted to use for something other than faithful worship of Him?
Judah kept the right city and the right temple yet still built high places and Asherah poles. Where might you be relying on right structures or right associations while your own heart quietly drifts?
The death of Jeroboam's son came exactly as Ahijah said it would. How does the certainty of God's word, for both warning and promise, shape the way you face hard news today?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote explains that 'every male' renders a Hebrew idiom literally meaning all those who urinate against a wall, a blunt ancient expression for every male person, without softening the force of the original.
The BSB footnote notes 'will receive a proper burial' is literally 'who will come to the grave,' a Hebrew idiom for an honorable burial in contrast to the rest of the household.
The BSB footnote notes 'the Euphrates' renders the Hebrew idiom 'the River,' the standard way the Euphrates is referenced in this period.
The BSB footnote notes Abijam is a variant of Abijah in some Hebrew manuscripts and the Septuagint, see 2 Chronicles 12:16, the same king named Abijam through 1 Kings 15.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 14:1-31. One em dash in the source text of verse 14, reading 'This is the day, yes, even today!' with a dash before 'yes,' was converted to a comma so no dash characters remain; no other dash characters appear anywhere in this file.
